senses.definition: a curve that separates different regions of motion in a dynamical system, often representing a boundary between distinct behaviors in phase space
examples: In the chaotic system, trajectories near the separatrix show dramatically different outcomes.
The separatrix divides stable and unstable motion in the mathematical model.

senses.definition: a term used in historical or taxonomic contexts to denote something that is separated or detached; also appears as a genus or specimen label in some classifications
examples: The separatum was stored separately from the main collection.
In the old manuscript, separatum items were noted in the margin.

senses.definition: plural of separatum; items or elements that are separated or set apart in a collection or classification
examples: The repository contained several separatums from different periods.
Researchers compared the separatums to understand variation.

senses.definition: to separate or set apart (archaic or rare spelling variant of separate)
examples: To separe this grain from the chaff requires care.
The ritual separe the two groups.

senses.definition: to transplant or place a pawn-like element to a new location, often in gaming or planting contexts
examples: The player decided to sepawn the piece to a safer square.
In horticulture, sepawned saplings were placed near the garden edge.

senses.definition: an abbreviation, potentially standing for a technical term depending on context
examples: Refer to the sepd protocol specification.
The dataset uses sepd to denote separator in logs.

senses.definition: an abbreviation that may denote a specific technical term in a given field
examples: Configure sepg settings before deployment.
The sepg flag controls the feature toggle.

senses.definition: a historical or literary term referring to the Jewish diaspora or to the land of Spain in some medieval Jewish sources
examples: Sepharad is often used in religious texts to denote Spain or the Jewish exile.
Scholars discuss Sepharad in the context of medieval Jewish history.

senses.definition: a member of the Jewish people traditionally descended from the Jewish communities of the Iberian Peninsula (especially Spain and Portugal)
examples: Sephardi communities have distinct liturgical traditions.
She studied Sephardi history and culture.

senses.definition: relating to Sephardi Jews or their culture, traditions, or language
examples: Sephardi rites are different from Ashkenazi rites.
She wore Sephardi jewelry at the celebration.

senses.definition: relating to Sephardi Jews or their culture and traditions
examples: Sephardic music blends Mediterranean influences.
She collects Sephardic recipes.

senses.definition: plural form referring to Sephardi Jews or communities
examples: The Sephardim have preserved many unique customs.
Sephardim communities flourished in the Mediterranean.

senses.definition: a person belonging to the Sepharvite subgroup within Sephardi Jews
examples: The Sepharvites contributed to the trade routes of the era.
Scholars discuss the traditions of the Sepharvites.

senses.definition: a traditional or religious term possibly referring to a calendar, ritual, or spiritual concept in certain contexts
examples: The priest recited a sephen during the ceremony.
In ancient texts, a sephen denotes a sacred season.

senses.definition: plural form of sepen/sephen in historical or religious contexts; refers to multiple instances of a sephen
examples: The manuscript lists several sephens across the year.
Scholars note variations among the sephens.

senses.definition: a divine emanation or attribute in Kabbalistic tradition, often part of the Tree of Life (Sefirot)
examples: The sephira of Wisdom is one of the Sefirot.
Students study how the sephira concept appears in mysticism.

senses.definition: alternate spelling of sephira, referring to a divine emanation in Jewish mysticism
examples: Each sephirah represents a different aspect of God.
The study traced the symbolism of the seirah in liturgy.

senses.definition: relating to Sephirot or to the mystical emanations in Kabbalah
examples: The sephiric correspondences are central to the mystic framework.
Sephiric symbolism appears in some medieval texts.

senses.definition: plural of sephira; the ten emanations in the Jewish Kabbalistic Tree of Life
examples: The ten sephiroth form the structure of the mystical map.
Each sephiroth has its own traits and energies.

senses.definition: relating to the sephiroth or their attributes in Kabbalistic thought
examples: Sephirothic symbolism appears in the mystic diagrams.
They discussed sephirothic correspondences in the lecture.

senses.definition: a brownish-gray color like old photographs; or a photo with that tone
examples: The old album had sepia photographs.
She tinted the image to sepia for a vintage look.

senses.definition: having a brownish, reddish-yellow color like sepia
examples: He wore sepia-colored glasses.
The sepia paper gave the sketch a warm look.

senses.definition: resembling or relating to cuttlefish or their appearance
examples: The sepiacean hues suggested underwater life.
Sepiacacean textures mimic ocean patterns.

senses.definition: of or relating to sepia or the sepia tone; also used as a noun for a sepia-colored thing
examples: The sepian photograph carries a vintage charm.
He printed sepian images for the project.

senses.definition: the plural form of sepia in counting or cataloging colors or prints
examples: The exhibit displayed several sepiae from different eras.
Collectors valued the sepiae for their warmth.

senses.definition: resembling sepia in color or tone
examples: The sepialike photograph added a vintage mood.
They used sepialike filters on the video.

senses.definition: a person who favors or uses sepia tones in art or photography; or a hypothetical inhabitant of sepia-toned environments
examples: The sepian preferred classic, warm tones in his portraits.
In the gallery, several sepian works drew attention.
